coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as heterojunction transport mediators effectively enhance BiOI/g-C3N4 synergistic persulfate degradation of antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chao
Liu, Yali
Lv, Xinya
Lv, Shuyi
cheng, Han
Kang, Xiaorong
Li, Xin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a ternary structured photocatalyst carbon dots (CDs) modified BiOI/g-C3N4 (CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4) was prepared by a simple one-pot in situ coprecipitation method using ethylene glycol as chelating agent. Under LED irradiation, CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4, especially in coordination with persulfate (PS), exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance and cycling stability for tetracycline (TC) mineralization. Even after five cycles, the removal rate of TC still remained around 80%. Meanwhile, the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity and its photogenerated carriers transfer was elucidated by optical, electrochemical and energy band structure characterization. The deformation of band structure made the interfaces of g-C3N4 and BiOI generate Schottky barrier with CDs, respectively. CDs acted as an electron transfer and storage medium, facilitating the separation and migration of electron-hole pairs (e−-h+) at the interface of the heterojunction structure. The photocatalytic experiments with PS showed that the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS advanced oxidation system achieved 82.7% removal of TC, which was 6.7, 3.1, and 1.2 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4, BiOI, and BiOI/g-C3N4 photocatalysts. The degradation of TC intermediates was further discussed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
BiOI/g-C3N4
Persulfate
Tetracycline
Coupling interface

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BiOI and CDs@ BiOI/g-C3N4
Roles of PS in the TC degradation
Effect of operating parameters on TC removal

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51808282 ), and Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province, China ( SJCX21_0335 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4.
XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of all as-prepared samples.
XPS survey spectra (a), Bi 4f (b), I 3d (c), C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e), and O 1 s (f) for CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4.
SEM images of g-C3N4 (a), BiOI (b), CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 (c and d), HRTEM images of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 (e and f).
The UV–Vis DRS (a) and the band gaps (b) of all as-prepared samples.
PL spectra (a), transient photocurrent response (b), and EIS (c) of g-C3N4, BiOI/g-C3N4 and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4.

TC removal in individual photocatalytic systems (a) and photocatalyst/PS systems (b) under LED; the corresponding kinetic linear simulation curves in individual photocatalytic systems (c) and photocatalyst/PS systems (d).
Degradation efficiencies of TC in the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system under different conditions: (a) CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 dosage, (b) PS concentration, (c) initial TC concentration, and (d) system stability.
ESR spectra of OH and SO4 - (a); O2 – (b); h+ (c) over CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system in the dark or under visible-light; Effects of radical scavengers on the degradation of TC (d).
Possible photocatalytic mechanism for TC degradation over the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system.
LC-MS spectra of BiOI/g-C3N4/PS (a) and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS (b) systems.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as heterojunction transport mediators effectively enhance BiOI/g-C3N4 synergistic persulfate degradation of antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Engineering, Nanjing Institute of Technology, Nanjing 211167, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Engineering, Nanjing Institute of Technology, Nanjing 211167, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150090, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a ternary structured photocatalyst carbon dots (CDs) modified BiOI/g-C3N4 (CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4) was prepared by a simple one-pot in situ coprecipitation method using ethylene glycol as chelating agent. Under LED irradiation, CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4, especially in coordination with persulfate (PS), exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance and cycling stability for tetracycline (TC) mineralization. Even after five cycles, the removal rate of TC still remained around 80%. Meanwhile, the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity and its photogenerated carriers transfer was elucidated by optical, electrochemical and energy band structure characterization. The deformation of band structure made the interfaces of g-C3N4 and BiOI generate Schottky barrier with CDs, respectively. CDs acted as an electron transfer and storage medium, facilitating the separation and migration of electron-hole pairs (e−-h+) at the interface of the heterojunction structure. The photocatalytic experiments with PS showed that the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS advanced oxidation system achieved 82.7% removal of TC, which was 6.7, 3.1, and 1.2 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4, BiOI, and BiOI/g-C3N4 photocatalysts. The degradation of TC intermediates was further discussed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, g-C3N4-based heterojunction photocatalysts utilizing visible light for efficient redox reactions have attracted extensive attention in the fields of CO2 emission reduction, clean energy manufacturing and micropollutant degradation [1–4]. The two-dimensional layered semiconductor structure of g-C3N4 is beneficial to shortening the transmission distance of photogenerated carriers and promoting charge separation and migration [5]. At the same time, it has the advantages of low cost, simple preparation, good response to visible light, and stable physical and chemical properties. However, the broad application of g-C3N4 is still greatly limited by the utilization of visible light [5–7].

To further improve the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4, various modification methods, including doping non-metallic elements [8,9], loading metals [10,11], introducing defective structures [12,13], and modifying morphologic structures [8,14] have been adopted. Among them, the integration of g-C3N4 with other energy-level-matched semiconductors to construct heterojunction structures is very popular [10]. In particular, the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction is considered to be an effective method, which not only inhibits photoelectron-hole recombination, but also maintains strong redox ability [15,16]. So far, many studies on Z-Scheme g-C3N4-based heterojunctions, such as NiTiO3/g-C3N4 [17], W18O49/Au/g-C3N4 [18], g-C3N4/WO3 [19], BiOBr/g-C3N4 [20], have shown excellent photocatalytic activities.

As we know, the separation of electron-hole pair (e--h+), photogenerated carriers migration rate, and optical absorption capacity are the key factors affecting photocatalytic activity. It has been reported that BiOI is an anisotropic layered structure composed of [Bi2O2]2+ and double I layers, and its 3D rose ball-like structure is beneficial for the formation of internal electric fields and the migration of photogenerated carriers [21,22]. Simultaneously, BiOI has a very narrow forbidden band (1.8–2.0 eV), which implies a considerable light absorption range from the visible to infrared spectral region [23]. Coupled with stable chemical properties and matching energy levels with g-C3N4 [20], BiOI is being applied in the research of energy-band engineering [24,25]. However, the hybridization of Bi 6 s orbitals and O 2p orbitals leads to a high rate of photogenerated e--h+ complexation [23]. Therefore, how to endow g-C3N4 with sufficient active sites and higher separation efficiency of photogenerated e--h+ is very important. Recently, g-C3N4 was compounded with BiOI to construct Z-scheme heterojunctions via energy band engineering, resulting in stronger redox ability and higher e--h+ separation efficiency [26–29].

Even so, there is still much room for improvement in the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4-based heterojunctions. Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of nanomaterials composed of discrete quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles ranging from 2 to10 nm, have attracted much attention due to their nontoxicity and good e- transfer properties [30]. More importantly, CDs have remarkable up-conversion capability to convert low-energy photons into high-energy photons with ultra-long-lived photogenerated carriers, effectively utilizing the full spectrum of sunlight [30]. Due to their unique microstructure and optical properties, CDs have been widely applied in multiphase optics, such as biological detection [31], photosensitizers [32], metal salt reduction [33], enhanced adsorption capacity [34], and spectral converters [35]. It is noteworthy that g-C3N4-based Z-scheme heterojunctions loaded with CDs could significantly improve their photocatalytic activity [36].

In addition to improving the structural defects of the material itself by energy band engineering, advanced oxidation techniques combining photocatalytic systems with persulfate (PS) is also a research hotspot in recent years [37,38]. PS can be activated by photocatalysts to produce sulfate radicals (SO4 -, 2.5–3.0 eV) with strong oxidizing power[37]. Its oxidation capacity is similar to that of hydroxyl radicals (OH, 2.8 eV), and its duration (25–40 µs) is longer than OH (5–10 ns). Moreover, it has a wide pH adaptation range (2 ∼ 8) and high selectivity for organic compounds containing unsaturated bonds and aromatic structures [39]. On the other hand, PS can be fed back into the photocatalyst, using the oxidative nature of oxyanions as e- trap to reduce the recombination of e--h+ pairs, thereby improving the performance of the photocatalytic system [40]. Nevertheless, the effect of PS-coordinated CDs on Z-scheme heterojunction of BiOI/g-C3N4 is not clear.

In this paper, a combination of thermal polymerization and in situ deposition was used to construct a CDs-loaded ternary heterojunction photocatalyst (CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4). Based on the characterization results, the formation mechanism of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 was proposed. Furthermore, the effectiveness of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 in synergy with PS (CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS) system was investigated with antibiotic tetracycline (TC) as the target contaminant and LED as the light source, simultaneously, the active species during the reaction were detected and distinguished. The contribution of CDs to the mineralization of TC macromolecular intermediates was obtained by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) spectroscopy.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) and potassium iodide (KI) were provided by Shanghai Mack-Lin Biochemical Technology Co. The urea, melamine, potassium persulfate, ammonium oxalate (AO), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), citric acid, 4-benzoquinone (BQ), and methyl alcohol (MeOH) used in this experiment were all analytically pure, and they were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, Nanjing, China.

Traditional g-C3N4 samples were synthesized through thermal polymerization process [41]. Typically, 3 g melamine was placed in an alumina crucible and wrapped in tinfoil. Next, it was calcined in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 to 550 °C (1.5 h), and then cooled to room temperature. Finally, the g-C3N4 block was ground into powder and sieved.

According to previous literature, CDs were prepared using a simple hydrothermal reaction [42]. Firstly, 3 g urea and 1 g citric acid were added to 20 mL of deionized water. Then, the mixed solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave, which was heated to 180 °C for 15 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the clear solution in the solution was collected, and the impurities in the solution were further removed by ultra-centrifugation (10,000 rpm), and then placed in a dialysis bag equipped with a molecular cut-off of 500 for 3 d. The desired solution of CDs was finally obtained and stored at low temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BiOI and CDs@ BiOI/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: BiOI/g-C3N4 was prepared by an in-situ deposition method [26]. First, 0.071 g of KI and 0.05 g of g-C3N4 were added to 10 mL of deionized water and stirred magnetically to form solution A. In another beaker, 0.206 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 15 mL of ethylene glycol were added and sonicated for 30 min to form solution B. Then, the solution B was slowly added drop by drop to the solution A being magnetically stirred. Finally, after stirring for 3 h, the orange-red precipitate was obtained by centrifugation (4000 rpm), which was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, and then dried overnight in an oven at 70 °C.

CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 was prepared by adding 1 mL of CDs solution to solution A 10 min before the drop of solution B into solution A (Fig. 1 ). The other steps were the same as the preparation of BiOI/g-C3N4.

Surface functional groups and chemical composition distributions were characterized by Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (XRD, RIKEN, Japan), VERTEX 80 V Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker, Germany), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Shimadzu, Japan). Structural external characterization included a Regulus 8100 cold field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan), a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, Japan), and a specific surface area aperture analyzer (BET, ASAP2020 HD88, Micromeritics, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra with excitation wavelength of 380 nm were detected by Varian Cary (FLS980, UK); ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured by LAMBDA 950 spectrometer (LAMBDA 950, PE, USA) with BaSO4 as reference; electrochemistry was performed in 0.5 M Na2SO4 using a Chennai 660 workstation to record the photoelectric current (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS).

The photocatalytic degradation of 20 mg/L TC aqueous solution was carried out under a 30 W LED light source, and a certain amount of photocatalyst and PS.Before LED light irradiation, the suspension was stirred in darkness for 30 min to ensure the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of TC on the photocatalyst.After that, PS was added according to the experimental conditions. During the experiment, 2 mL sample was periodically withdrawn and filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane to analyze TC concentration by high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC, Waters).TC-degraded intermediates were detected by LC-MS.

The kinetic curve of TC photocatalytic degradation was fitted by the following formula (Eq.1):
(1)
- ln
C
C 0
= k t

In the formula,
C
(mg/L) was the TC concentration at t,
C 0
(mg/L) was the initial TC concentration,
k
(min−1) was the reaction rate constant, and t (min) was the reaction time.

To detect the role of active substances in the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system for TC degradation, some active substance quenching agents were added to the TC solution. Specifically, BQ, TAB, and AO were used to scavenge superoxide radical (O2 –), OH, and h+, respectively, while MeOH acted as a common scavenger for OH and SO4 -. Electron spin resonance (ESR): central magnetic field was 3500.00 g;Sweep width was 150.00 G;the sweep time was 30.00 s;the microwave power was 3.99 mW;the modulation amplitude was 1.000 G;conversion time and frequency were 40.0 ms and 9.854854 GHz, respectively.

The phase structures of the g-C3N4, BiOI, BiOI/g-C3N4, and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, there were two characteristic peaks at about 13°and 27° corresponding to lattice planes of (110) and (002) of g-C3N4, which were ascribed to the structure of the triazine ring and the conjugated aromatic system, respectively (JCPDS Card NO. 87–1526) [41,43]. Also, the diffraction peaks of pure BiOI sample perfectly correspond to the BiOI standard card (JCPDS NO. 10–0445), where the diffraction peaks at 30.08° and 31.89° corresponding to the (102) and (110) crystal planes were obvious [27,28,44]. The diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 and BiOI were observed in the XRD patterns of BiOI/g-C3N4, indicating the successful combination of these two semiconductors. Moreover, CDs loading onto BiOI/g-C3N4 only slightly affected the intensity of the diffraction peak (102). This result indicated that the introduction of CDs contributed to the layered crystal structure of BiOI/g-C3N4, but did not change the overall phase structure.

The FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4, BiOI, BiOI/g-C3N4 and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 were given in Fig. 2b. For all as-prepared samples, broad absorption peaks in the range of 3000–3600 cm−1 were designated as stretching vibrations of –OH/NH groups, which were associated with amino groups and surface-adsorbed H2O molecules, respectively [27,45]. In the FTIR of g-C3N4, the absorption peaks of 1241 cm−1, 1410 cm−1, and 1633 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of CN and CN heterocycles, respectively, and the sharp peak at 806 cm−1 belonged to the bending vibration of breathing mode of triazine units [46]. For pure BiOI, Bi had no typical absorption peak in FT-IR spectrum, while characteristic peaks at 631 cm−1 and 503 cm−1 correspond to the BiO stretching vibration modes [47]. There were no significant differences between the FT-IR spectra of BiOI/g-C3N4 and CDs@ BiOI/g-C3N4, indicating that the binding strength between BiOI and g-C3N4 was not weakened by CDs [48]. This might be due to the small amount of CDs addition.

Furthermore, the bonding structure, surface chemical composition and elemental state of the as-prepared materials were analyzed by XPS. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a, CDs@ BiOI/g-C3N4 was mainly composed of C, N, Bi, O and I elements without any impurities. The XPS spectra were calibrated with the C 1 s characteristic peak (284.8 eV) as the standard.

In Fig. 3b, the peak between 155 eV and 161 eV was metallic Bi in binary and ternary hybridizations, while the stronger characteristic peak between 162 eV and 166 eV was attributed to BiO bond in the BiOI lattice, which could be indexed by standard Bi3+ ions (Bi 4f7/2, Bi 4f5/2). The I 3d corresponding XPS spectrum of the sample had two symmetric peaks centered at 618.8 eV and 630 eV for I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively, indicating the negative monovalent state of I (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, the C1s binding energy ranged from 282.8 eV to 292.9 eV, which could be divided into three different characteristic peaks concentrated at 284.8 eV, 288.7 eV and 286.4 eV, corresponding to sp2 (CC), sp2 (NNC) of g-C3N4, and CO of CDs particles, respectively. In Fig. 3e, the N1s spectra of CDs@ BiOI/g-C3N4 fitted into two peaks, corresponding to the CNC group and the N(C)3 group at 398.5 eV and 400.95 eV respectively. The O 1 s peak could be decomposed into three peaks on CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4. The fitted peak around 529.8 eV was associated with the lattice oxygen atom (BiO), and the peak position around 531.8 eV corresponded to the OH group of adsorbed water. The third peak at 533.2 eV could be attributed to CO contaminants on the surface (Fig. 3f).

Based on the above band identification and description, the elements Bi, I and N correspond perfectly to pure BiOI and g-C3N4 [49,50], but the XPS with respect to C1s and O1s changed obviously due to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups at CDs. Compared with the pure catalyst, the characteristic peaks intensities of CO, OH and CO were more pronounced [21,51]. It could be concluded that ternary heterojunction was successfully formed.

The SEM and HRTEM images of samples were shown in Fig. 4 . The pure g-C3N4 nanosheets were twisted together to present large-scale sheet-like structures with a width of 15–20 nm (Fig. 4a). For the pure BiOI in Fig. 4b, a more regular and uniform 3D rose ball structure could be clearly observed, which was composed of uniform nanoplates with a diameter of 3–5 µm and a thickness of 20 nm. The lamellar structure of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 exhibited the advantages shared by g-C3N4 and BiOI, having a uniform and smaller nanoflake structure, and cleaving from a rose-like to a maple leaf shape (Fig. 4c). In addition, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) patterns of C, N, Bi, O and I in the samples were used to demonstrate the successful preparation of the ternary CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 hybrid composites.

As shown in Fig. 4d, the BiOI nanoflakes were deposited on the surface of g-C3N4, and ultrasonic waves could not separate them during the sampling process of HRTEM measurements, proving that BiOI and g-C3N4 were tightly bound. As can be seen from the HRTEM image in Fig. 4e, nanosheets of different transparency were bonded together, where the nanosheets with higher transparency belonged to g-C3N4, and the other dark-colored nanosheets were BiOI. To prove its authenticity, the lattices of both substances were indicated (Fig. 4f).

The pore structures of all prepared samples are shown in Table S1. The specific surface areas and total pore volumes of these four catalysts followed the order: CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 > BiOI/g-C3N4 > BiOI > g-C3N4. With the loading of CDs, the specific surface area of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 reached 41.374 m2/g, which was helpful to improve the overall adsorption performance of photocatalyst to some extent. The significant increase in the specific surface area of BiOI/g-C3N4 probably because the 3D lamellar structure of BiOI was more regular and homogeneous than g-C3N4, and the heterojunction formed by the complexation of BiOI and g-C3N4 solved the agglomeration problem of g-C3N4.

Fig. 5 a showed the UV–Vis-DRS profiles of g-C3N4, BiOI, BiOI/g-C3N4 and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4. The absorbances of all photocatalytic samples were in the visible region. The basic absorption edge of g-C3N4 appeared near 470 nm, having a wider band gap and poorer absorbance performance. In contrast, BiOI possessed a stronger absorbance performance around 627 nm. It is worth noting that the absorbance property of compositely constructed BiOI/g-C3N4 was closer to that of BiOI than g-C3N4, exhibiting a clearer red shift and stronger visible light response. In addition, a slight improvement of the visible response was observed after BiOI/g-C3N4 loading with CDs. That might be explained by the fact that g-C3N4 could absorb the short waves converted by CDs, but BiOI could not fully utilize the up-conversion function of CDs.

The forbidden bandwidth gap was calculated by Tauc-Plot equation (Eq. (2)):
(2)
(
α h v )
2 n
= A
( h v -
E g
)

According to the above formula, the band gap map was drawn with (αhν) 1/2 as ordinate and hν as abscissa (Fig. 5b). The band gap energy values of g-C3N4, BiOI, BiOI/g-C3N4 and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 were 2.79, 1.86, 2.23 and 2.17 eV, respectively, which were in line with the above-mentioned change rule of DRS map.The band gap of g-C3N4 was relatively wider, while a very narrow heterojunction gap was formed after recombination with BiOI. It was conducive to the transition of photogenerated carriers from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) and the generation of photogenerated e--h+ pairs. Although the up-conversion capability of CDs was not fully utilized, its charge storage capability still shortened the distance for photoelectron migration.

Furthermore, the CV and VB positions of pure g-C3N4 and BiOI samples were estimated by the Eq. (3) and Eq. (4):
(3)
E
VB
= X -
E e
+ 0.5
E g
(4)
E
CB
=
E
VB
-
E g

Here, X was the absolute electronegativity of semiconductor photocatalyst (g-C3N4: 4.7 eV; BiOI: 6.1 eV), Ee represented the energy of free e- on the hydrogen scale (−4.5 eV), Eg was the forbidden band gap of semiconductor photocatalyst (g-C3N4: 2.79 eV; BiOI: 1.98 eV). Based on these equations, the EVB and ECB of g-C3N4 were calculated to be 1.59 eV and −1.20 eV, respectively, and the corresponding EVB and ECB of BiOI were 2.53 eV and 0.67 eV.

The separation and migration properties of photogenerated e--h+ pairs are crucial for subsequent photocatalytic applications. Therefore, to further verify the photoelectric properties of the pure, binary, and ternary samples, the e--h+ pairs of the samples were qualitatively and quantitatively analyzed by PL test (Fig. 6 a). All samples had a broad emission peak in the range of 400–550 nm. However, the luminescence intensity decreased after the deposition of CDs and/or BiOI on the g-C3N4, and the intensity of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 nanocomposites was lowest compared with other samples. It could be concluded that the deposited CDs and BiOI nanoparticles played an important role in hindering the rapid complexation of charge carriers on g-C3N4.

Moreover, the powder samples in this study were prepared as thin-film electrodes, and their photocurrent and resistance were tested using an electrochemical workstation. Fig. 6b showed the transient photocurrent density-time curves of g-C3N4, BiOI/g-C3N4, and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 working electrodes during several intermittent optical cycles under simulated visible light illumination. Each switching period of the pulse was 40 s. It is clear to see that CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 had the highest photocurrent density, followed by BiOI and g-C3N4, indicating that it had a good ability to generate e--h+ pairs.The improved photocurrent response should be attributed to the synergistic effect of CDs charge transfer and interfacial heterojunction. Specifically, the capture, migration and storage of photogenerated electrons by CDs, as well as the unique charge field of heterojunctions, inhibited the recombination of e--h+ pairs in ternary nanocomposites CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4.

For a more intuitive analysis of the charge transport ability, the EIS Nyquist plots of g-C3N4, BiOI/ g-C3N4 and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 were discussed (Fig. 6c). It is well known that the smaller the arc radius, the smaller the charge transfer resistance. In this study, the smallest arc radius observed in the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 demonstrated that this complex had the lowest electron transport impedance and the best conductivity. This result was consistent with PL and PC analysis. In summary, CDs-semiconductor structure and heterojunction structure effectively promoted e--h+ pairs separation and photoinduced charge migration, and further improved the photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Roles of PS in the TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts and/or PS system for TC degradation under LED irradiation were investigated. The main control parameters were as follows: pH of 7, catalyst concentration of 0.35 g/L, PS concentration of 4 mM, and TC concentration of 20 mg/L. As shown in Fig. 7 a, TC was relatively stable in the aqueous environment without any photocatalyst. The TC removal rate did not increase significantly after adding PS, indicating that PS could not be fully activated by LED light. When g-C3N4, BIOI and BIOI/g-C3N4 were introduced, the TC removal rates reached 12.3%, 26.6% and 48.3%, respectively, and the corresponding k values were 0.002, 0.004 and 0.012 (Fig. 7c). In the single CDs@BIOI/g-C3N4 system, the TC removal rate and k value were further increased to 66.3% and 0.018, which was 5.39, 2.49 and 1.37 times higher than that of g-C3N4, BIOI and BIOI/g-C3N4.

In addition, the adsorption–desorption rates of BiOI/g-C3N4 and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 in the dark reaction reached 18.6% and 26.7%, indicating that the ternary complex of g-C3N4, BiOI and CDs indeed contributed to the adsorption of TC molecules on the surface of the 2D sheet for next and electron reactions. In the systems of g-C3N4/PS, BiOI/PS, BiOI/g-C3N4/PS and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS, the TC removal rate increased to 29.6%, 38.4%, 68.3% and 82.7%, respectively (Fig. 7b), and the corresponding k values enhanced to 0.006, 0.007, 0.021, and 0.029. These results proved that as-prepared photocatalysts and PS produced a synergistic degradation effect on TC, and PS played a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation process. In the previous literature, a similar TC removal rate (86.1%) was observed nitrogen-doped g-C3N4/PS system, however, the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system in this study had a higher initial TC concentration and a lower catalyst dosage [S4]. Different from the Mn2O3/Bi2O3/PS and Co3O4/Bi4O7/Bi2O3/PS systems, TC degradation in this study was completed under LED light irradiation, which was safer and more economical than xenon lamps and metal halide lamps[S6, S9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of operating parameters on TC removal
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal photocatalytic conditions of the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system for TC degradation were investigated, and the dosage of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4, PS concentration and initial TC concentration were the main parameters. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the removal rate of TC was positively correlated with the dosage of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 with its initial concentrations of 0.05–0.35 g/L. When the initial amount of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 increased from 0.35 to 0.45 g/L, the increment of TC removal rate changed slightly. Even when the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 dosage continuously increased to 0.65 g/L, a significant decrease of TC removal was observed. The decrease in TC degradation ability might be explained by the brownish-red with darker color of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4. The light transmission in the dispersion deteriorated with the increase of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 injection and the use of LED light source. In addition to that, the active sites were occupied and squeezed because of mutual adhesion. Thus, 0.35 g/L was considered as the optimal dosage and used for the subsequent experiments.

Fig. 8b showed the removal rate of TC at different initial PS concentrations. As the initial PS concentration increased from 0 to 8 mM, the TC removal rate increased first and then decreased. The maximum value occurred at 4 mM, showing that the optimal ratio of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 to PS existed under simulated solar illumination conditions. It might be because the S2O8 2- of PS, as an oxyanion, acted as an e- trap and effectively inhibited the recombination of e--h+ pairs. However, excessive PS led to the reaction of S2O8 2− with SO4 −, which had a certain negative effect on SO4 −, see Eq. 5 and Eq. 6.
(5) S2O8 2- + e-→SO4 - + SO4 2-
(6)
SO4 - +S2O8 2-→S2O8 - + SO4 2-

It can be seen from Fig. 8c that the TC removal rate decreased with the increase of initial TC concentrations. For example, TC was almost completely degraded within 60 min at an initial TC concentration of 10.0 mg/L, while 51.8% of TC remained in the solution after 60 min with an initial TC concentration of 30 mg/L. This might be attributed to the limited active groups generated by the fixed catalyst dosage.

The stability and reproducibility of CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS under LED irradiation was further investigated by TC photodegradation cycling test. As can be seen in Fig. 8d, the photodegradation rate of TC did not decrease significantly in the cycling test, and the photocatalytic rate remained around 80% in the fifth cycle, which verified the high stability of the photocatalytic system. At the same time, the XRD patterns of the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 samples before and after the photocatalytic reaction were analyzed (Fig.S1). It was found that the peak position of the used sample was shifted to a slightly higher angle than the fresh sample, which was due to the loss of ordered structure and the reduction of interlayer distance during the reaction process. Nevertheless, the crystal structure of the sample did not change after the reaction, indicating that the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 was relatively stable. In summary, it could be demonstrated that CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS was a stable system combining adsorption, catalysis, and activation, which helped to enhance its practical application.

The active substances in the system were detected by ESR technique. In the ESR tests, 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was commonly used as a radical spin trapping agents to confirm the presence of OH, SO4 - and O2 –, and the results were shown in Fig. 9 . It is worth noting that there was no ESR signal in the dark, as neither CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 nor PS could be activated in this case. After 5 min of visible light irradiation, the signals of DMPO-OH (labeled as “♦”), DMPO-SO4 - (labeled as “●”) and DMPO-O2 – showed specific characteristics.

To identify the main active substances in the photocatalytic process of TC by the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS, free radical and h+ scavenging experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 9d, the removal rates of TC decreased by 3.48% and 9.19% after adding 1 mM of TBA and MeOH to the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 system, respectively. This results suggested that OH and SO4 - were not the main active substances. After adding 1 mM AO and BQ, the TC removal rate decreased by 41.27% and 38.0%, respectively, indicating that h+ and O2 – were the dominant active substances.

Based on photoelectrochemical tests and free radical scavenging experiments, a photocatalytic mechanism of TC degradation by CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS was described, as shown in Fig. 10 . Under LED irradiation, the photogenerated carriers of BiOI and g-C3N4 were excited, and the e- and h+ were located on the CB and VB, respectively. When BiOI recombined with g-C3N4, a Z-scheme heterojunction was formed, allowing the e- to transfer between these two materials and eventually reached Fermi energy level equilibrium. The deformation of band structure made the interfaces of g-C3N4 and BiOI generate Schottky barrier with CDs, respectively. When it was exposed in LED light, the Schottky barrier prevented e- flowing back to BiOI. Meanwhile, the CDs could act as bridges in the electric field, shorten the migration distance of photogenerated electrons from BiOI (CB) to g-C3N4 (VB), and further prevent the recombination of e--h+ pairs inside g-C3N4. As a result, the photogenerated e- and h+ were concentrated in the CB of g-C3N4 and VB of BiOI, respectively. O2 was reduced by e- to O2 – with strong oxidizing power, which was one of the reasons why the material itself could efficiently remove TC. It is stated that the standard redox potential of O2/O2 – is about −0.33 eV, which is lower than the CB of BiOI (0.67 eV) and higher than that of g-C3N4 (−1.20 eV). Therefore, when the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 was illuminated under the LED light, the photogenerated e- at CB of g-C3N4 would reduce O2 toO2 –, which was consistent with the charge flow direction of the Z-type heterostructure.

After adding a small amount of PS with higher redox potential, the photogenerated e- in the CB of g-C3N4 and BiOI could easily react with PS, leading to the cleavage of OO bond to form SO4 -. In addition, the surface e--h+ recombination was effectively suppressed due to the e- depletion in BiOI (CB) by PS, which allowed more h+ on BiOI (VB) to participate in the oxidative degradation of TC molecules. According to the above discussion and active substances analysis, the whole photocatalytic process was stated as follows:
g-C3N4 h+ (g-C3N4) + e− (g-C3N4)
BiOI h+ (BiOI) + e− (BiOI)
S2O8 2− + e− (g-C3N4) → SO4 − + SO4 2−
S2O8 2− + e− (BiOI) → SO4 − + SO4 2−
H2O + SO4 − → OH + SO4 2− + H+
e− (BiOI) → h+ (CDs)
e− (CDs) → h+ (g-C3N4)
e− (g-C3N4) + O2 → O2 −
O2 −/ h+ (BiOI) + TC → byproducts

The intermediates in the photocatalytic reaction of BiOI/g-C3N4/PS and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS were analyzed by LC-MS, and the degradation mechanism of TC was further resolved. The mass spectra of TC intermediates after the reaction of BiOI/g-C3N4/PS and CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS systems for30 min were shown in Fig. 11 . The TC mass spectra in positive ion mode showed two characteristic peaks, m/z = 445.16 for [M + Na - CH2]+ and m/z = 467.14 for [M + Na]+. In both mass spectra, the TC (m/z = 445, 467) peaks decreased, and the two more prominent peaks (m/z = 301 and 55) increased, which were obtained after TC ring opening and demethylation. In the BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system, the characteristic peak intensity of TC-1 (m/z = 445) decreased, while the peak position of TC-2 (m/z = 467) remained relatively high. This indicated that the macromolecule TC-2 (m/z = 467) was not effectively removed. Compared to BiOI/g-C3N4/PS, the peak level of TC-2 (m/z = 467) in the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system significantly decreased, simultaneously, the peak levels of the two intermediates with relatively small mass nuclei of m/z = 301 and 55 increased. Due to the loading of CDs, CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS promoted the separation of e--h+ pairs, contributing to the oxidative removal of TC-2 (m/z = 467) macromolecule. Besides, carboxyl and hydroxyl reactive functional groups were attached to the surface of CDs, which could effectively attack TC, destroy their carbon chains, and further mineralize.

In this study, the ternary structured CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 was successfully prepared by loading CDs onto Z-scheme heterojunction BiOI/g-C3N4, and it exhibited good dark adsorption and photocatalytic performance for TC than the pristine BiOI/g-C3N4 under LED illumination. Simultaneously, a kinetic reaction model for photocatalytic degradation of TC by CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 was developed and validated through batch experiments. Advanced oxidation technology coupling with CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4 and PS was proved to be a stable system for efficient removal of TC. ESR tests and active substance quencher experiments showed that h+ and O2 – dominated the CDs@BiOI/g-C3N4/PS system. CDs as e- mediator and PS as electron trap greatly promoted the separation and migration of e--h+ pairs. LS-MS/MS tests demonstrated that the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs provided abundant active sites, which could effectively promote the de-cyclization and de-methylation of TC macromolecular intermediates.

Chao Yin: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yali Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Xinya Lv: Visualization, Investigation. Shuyi Lv: Visualization, Investigation. Han cheng: Supervision. Xiaorong Kang: Supervision. Xin Li: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51808282), and Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province, China (SJCX21_0335).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154249.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
